items.subtopic: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW
items.title: Thrombosis: A Major Cause of Morbidity and Mortality
items.content: Thrombosis, defined as hemostasis occurring at the wrong place or time, leads to significant morbidity and mortality across various arterial and venous diseases. It results in tissue anoxia and damage due to blood flow obstruction by thrombus formation.
keyPoints: Thrombosis is a major cause of morbidity and mortality.
In 2020, heart disease caused about 1 in 4 deaths among Americans (655,000 deaths).
Coronary disease resulted in 382,820 deaths; stroke led to 160,747 deaths in the U.S. in 2020.
Approximately 805,000 people suffered from heart attacks and 795,000 had strokes in 2020.
Pulmonary embolism or deep-venous thrombotic events affect about 600,000 people annually.
60,000–80,000 Americans die of pulmonary embolism or deep-venous thrombosis each year.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW

items.subtopic: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW
items.title: Pathophysiology of Hemostasis vs. Thrombosis
items.content: Hemostasis involves a balance between procoagulant and anticoagulant factors, which is crucial for preventing hemorrhage after injury. However, this same system can lead to pathological thrombosis under certain conditions, causing arterial or venous occlusion.
keyPoints: Physiologic hemostasis involves a delicate interplay between procoagulant and anticoagulant factors.
Pathological thrombosis occurs when normal hemostatic processes trigger vessel occlusion.
Thrombus formation is crucial in acute vascular diseases like myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular events, and venous thrombosis.
Venous thrombosis often involves hypercoagulable states due to coagulation/fibrinolysis defects or abnormalities in blood vessels/flow/stasis.
Arterial thrombosis is highly dependent on the vessel wall condition, platelets, and factors related to blood flow.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE
items.title: Mechanism of Arterial Thrombus Formation
items.content: Arterial thrombosis involves platelets adhering to the vessel wall, recruitment of additional platelets, and activation of thrombin. This process is crucial for understanding how arterial occlusion occurs.
keyPoints: Platelet adhesion, activation, aggregation, and recruitment are key steps in arterial thrombus formation.
The role of platelets extends beyond hemostasis to include immunity, metastasis, wound healing, and inflammation.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE
items.title: Burden of Arterial Thrombosis in the United States
items.content: Arterial thrombosis is a significant cause of morbidity and mortality, contributing to heart disease and stroke. Despite declining rates, it remains a major health issue.
keyPoints: Heart disease causes about 1 in every 4 deaths in the U.S.
Approximately 605,000 Americans experience a new coronary event annually.
An additional 200,000 myocardial infarctions occur in those with previous heart attacks.
About 795,000 people have a new or recurrent ischemic stroke each year.
In 2018, 1 in 6 deaths from cardiovascular disease were due to stroke.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.title: Platelet Life Cycle and Regulation
items.content: Platelets are small, anucleate cells derived from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. They circulate at concentrations of 200–400,000/µL with a lifespan of 7–10 days. Thrombopoietin (TPO) is the primary regulator of platelet formation.
keyPoints: Platelets are disc-shaped cells measuring 1–5 µm in diameter.
Circulating concentration: 200–400,000/µL.
Average lifespan: 7–10 days.
Derived from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow.
Thrombopoietin (TPO) regulates platelet formation.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.title: Mechanism of Platelet Adhesion
items.content: Platelet adhesion to a damaged vessel wall is initiated by exposure of subendothelial components like collagen and von Willebrand factor. The GPIb-IX-V complex binds to von Willebrand factor, facilitating platelet adherence and activation.
keyPoints: Adhesion begins with exposure of subendothelial components (e.g., collagen, von Willebrand factor).
GPIb-IX-V complex is central to platelet adhesion and activation.
Binding of GPIb-IX-V to von Willebrand factor triggers a calcium-dependent conformational change in the GPIIb/IIIa receptor.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.title: Platelet Activation and Receptor Function
items.content: Platelet activation involves surface receptors that regulate internal signaling pathways and binding to other proteins. Key receptors include the seven transmembrane receptor family, with ADP, thrombin, and prostaglandin receptors playing significant roles.
keyPoints: Activation is controlled by surface receptors regulating internal signaling and protein binding.
Seven transmembrane receptor family includes ADP, thrombin, and prostaglandin receptors.
PAR1, PAR2, and PAR4 are thrombin-activated receptors on platelets.
ADP-induced aggregation requires activation of P2Y12 and P2Y1 receptors.
Clopidogrel and prasugrel inhibit ADP-induced platelet aggregation.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.title: Role of Platelets in Thrombosis and Inflammation
items.content: Platelets play a crucial role in thrombosis during inflammatory processes, particularly in acute coronary syndromes. They interact with leukocytes via P-selectin to form heterotypic aggregates, enhancing immune responses but also promoting further platelet interactions through fibrinogen bridging.
keyPoints: P-selectin on activated platelets binds to PSGL-1 on leukocytes, forming platelet-leukocyte aggregates.
Increased CD11b/CD18 expression on leukocytes amplifies immunity and supports additional platelet interactions via bivalent fibrinogen linking with GPIIb/IIIa.
Platelet surface P-selectin induces tissue factor expression on monocytes, promoting fibrin formation.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.title: CD40 Ligand's Role in Thrombosis and Inflammation
items.content: The CD40 ligand on platelets, upon activation, is translocated to the surface and cleaved to form soluble CD40 ligand. This process links thrombosis with inflammation by contributing to both thrombotic events and atherosclerosis.
keyPoints: CD40 ligand is rapidly translocated to the platelet surface after stimulation.
Cleavage of CD40 ligand generates soluble CD40 ligand, linking thrombosis with inflammation.
Both CD40 and its ligand are involved in inflammatory processes leading to thrombosis and atherosclerosis.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.title: Platelets' Response to Infections and Their Role in Thrombosis
items.content: Infections, both bacterial and viral, can transiently increase the risk of thrombotic events by activating platelet responses. Platelets express Toll-like receptors (TLRs) that mediate these pro-inflammatory and thrombotic responses.
keyPoints: Bacterial infections activate platelets via TLR2 in a proinflammatory manner.
Viruses like SARS-CoV-2, HIV, hepatitis C virus, and Dengue elevate thrombosis risk through platelet receptors TLR7 and TLR8.
Platelets contribute to the pathophysiology of sepsis with high mortality rates.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS
items.title: Genetic Variants and Platelet Function in Arterial Thrombosis
items.content: Arterial thrombosis has been associated with genetic variants, though these associations are weak and not consistently confirmed. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified quantitative trait loci related to platelet count and mean platelet volume, including a locus at 12q24 linked to coronary artery disease risk.
keyPoints: Genetic associations with arterial thrombosis are weak and inconsistent.
GWAS identified 15 quantitative trait loci for platelet traits; one at 12q24 is also a coronary artery disease risk locus.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS
items.title: Pharmacogenetics of Antithrombotic Drugs
items.content: Genetic variability significantly affects individual responses to antithrombotic drugs, particularly aspirin and clopidogrel. Aspirin resistance is influenced by heritable factors, but no clinical genotyping recommendation exists for optimizing its use. Clopidogrel's effectiveness is affected by genetic variants in CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 genes, which impact drug metabolism.
keyPoints: Aspirin resistance has a heritable component; however, no firm evidence supports pharmacogenetic interactions with COX1 or other platelet receptors.
Clopidogrel responsiveness is affected by genetic variants in CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, accounting for 5–12% of variability in ADP-induced platelet aggregation.
Genetic factors do not significantly affect clinical outcomes with prasugrel or ticagrelor.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Incidence and Mortality of Venous Thromboembolism (VTE)
items.content: Venous thromboembolism, encompassing deep-vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE), is a significant health concern with notable incidence and mortality rates. DVT or PE occurs in approximately 1-2 individuals per 1000 annually, leading to 300,000-600,000 new cases each year in the United States. Mortality from these conditions is substantial, with around 60,000-80,000 deaths attributed to DVT or PE annually.
keyPoints: DVT or PE occurs in ~1-2 individuals per 1000 each year.
300,000-600,000 new cases of VTE annually in the United States.
Approximately 60,000-80,000 deaths attributed to DVT or PE annually.
Up to 30% of patients die within 30 days of a new VTE event.
One-fifth suffer sudden death due to PE.
30% develop recurrent VTE within 10 years.
9% 28-day fatality rate from DVT and 15% from PE (ARIC study).
25% fatality rate for PE in the setting of cancer.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Age-Related Incidence of DVT
items.content: The incidence of first deep-vein thrombosis (DVT) varies with age, showing a significant increase in older populations. The mean incidence is 5 per 10,000 person-years in the general population and increases from 2–3 per 10,000 at ages 30–49 to 20 per 10,000 at ages 70–79.
keyPoints: Mean incidence of first DVT: 5 per 10,000 person-years.
Incidence similar in males and females when adjusting for reproductive factors.
Increases with age: 2-3 per 10,000 (30-49 years) to 20 per 10,000 (70-79 years).
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Mechanisms of the Coagulation Cascade in Venous Thrombosis
items.content: The coagulation cascade is a series of enzymatic reactions crucial for hemostasis and thrombus formation. Initiated by vascular injury exposing tissue factor, it involves activation of factors VII, X, IX, and XI, leading to the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin and fibrinogen to fibrin. Thrombin also activates FXIII, stabilizing the clot. Antithrombotic factors like antithrombin and protein C regulate this process.
keyPoints: Coagulation cascade involves linked enzymatic reactions converting zymogens into active serine proteases.
Initiated by vascular injury exposing tissue factor.
Key steps: FVIIa activation, FX to FXa conversion, FIX activation, thrombin generation.
Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin and activates FXIII for clot stabilization.
Regulated by antithrombotic factors like antithrombin, TFPI, protein C/S.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Understanding Risk Factors and Genetic Predispositions for Venous Thrombosis
items.content: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) risk is influenced by a combination of genetic, acquired factors, and lifestyle conditions. Hypercoagulability due to genetics or other causes, immobilization, and venous stasis are primary contributors. Independent predictors for recurrence include age, obesity, malignancy, and acute extremity paresis. The prevalence of genetic predisposition in the U.S. is 5-8%. Major surgeries and cancer significantly elevate VTE risk, with hospitalized COVID-19 patients showing notable coagulation abnormalities.
keyPoints: Hypercoagulability from genetic or acquired factors contributes to VTE risk.
Independent predictors for recurrence include increasing age, obesity, malignancy, and acute extremity paresis.
5–8% of the U.S. population has a known genetic predisposition to venous thrombosis.
Cancer patients have approximately fourfold increased risk compared to the general population.
Hospitalized COVID-19 patients show nearly 20% coagulation abnormalities and increased VTE risk.
Risk of VTE doubles after air travel lasting 4 hours, with an absolute risk of 1 in 6000.
Relative risk of VTE among pregnant or postpartum women is 4.3; incidence is 199.7 per 100,000 woman-years.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Genetic Variants and Their Impact on Venous Thrombosis Risk
items.content: Certain genetic mutations significantly increase the risk of venous thrombosis. Heterozygous antithrombin deficiency and homozygosity for factor V Leiden mutation are notable contributors. While homozygous protein C or S deficiencies can lead to severe outcomes, heterozygous forms present a moderate risk. Activated protein C resistance due to factor V Leiden mutation is a key genetic risk factor. Polymorphisms in methylene tetrahydrofolate reductase and hyperhomocysteinemia are also independent risk factors, though their associations with thromboembolism are under scrutiny.
keyPoints: Heterozygous antithrombin deficiency and homozygosity of factor V Leiden mutation significantly increase VTE risk.
Homozygous protein C or S deficiencies can lead to fatal purpura fulminans; heterozygous forms have moderate thrombosis risk.
Factor V Leiden mutation causes resistance to activated protein C, increasing VTE risk.
Polymorphisms in methylene tetrahydrofolate reductase and hyperhomocysteinemia are independent risk factors for venous thrombosis.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Role of Fibrinolytic Abnormalities in Thrombosis
items.content: Specific abnormalities in the fibrinolytic system, such as elevated levels of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and plasminogen activator inhibitor type 1 (PAI-1), are associated with decreased fibrinolytic activity and increased risk of arterial thrombotic disease. The metabolic syndrome also contributes to altered fibrinolytic activity, promoting a procoagulant and hypofibrinolytic state.
keyPoints: Elevated levels of tPA and PAI-1 are associated with decreased fibrinolytic activity and increased risk of arterial thrombotic disease.
The 4G/5G insertion/deletion polymorphism in the PAI-1 gene is linked to decreased fibrinolytic activity.
Metabolic syndrome, characterized by central obesity, altered glucose metabolism, dyslipidemia, and hypertension, leads to a procoagulant and hypofibrinolytic state.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Initiation and Progression of Arterial vs. Venous Thrombosis
items.content: Venous thrombosis is primarily initiated by exposure to tissue factor in states of stasis or hypercoagulability, leading to thrombin formation and fibrin conversion. In contrast, arterial thrombosis is driven by platelet adhesion to injured vessels and exposure of extracellular matrix, with individual cellular predisposition playing a key role.
keyPoints: Venous thrombosis involves tissue factor exposure and the coagulation cascade leading to thrombin formation.
Arterial thrombosis is promoted by platelet adhesion and exposure of extracellular matrix.
Individual cellular predisposition significantly influences the propensity for arterial or venous thrombosis.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Hypercoagulability and Its Role in Thrombotic Events
items.content: While hypercoagulable states are well-established risk factors for venous thromboembolism (VTE), their role in arterial vascular disease is less clear. Specific thrombophilic conditions like factor V Leiden and prothrombin G20210A mutation are linked to DVT and PE but not strongly associated with arterial events.
keyPoints: Hypercoagulable states are significant risk factors for venous thromboembolism (VTE).
Factor V Leiden and prothrombin G20210A mutations are risk factors for DVT and PE, but their impact on arterial thrombosis is not well defined.
Common risk factors for both arterial and venous thrombosis include age, obesity, smoking, diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.question: What is the relative risk of VTE among pregnant or postpartum women?
items.explanation: The relative risk of venous thromboembolism among pregnant or postpartum women is 4.3.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.question: Which genetic polymorphism is associated with decreased fibrinolytic activity and increased risk of arterial thrombotic disease?
items.explanation: The 4G/5G polymorphism in the PAI-1 gene is associated with decreased fibrinolytic activity and increased risk of arterial thrombotic disease.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.question: What is a common feature of both arterial and venous thrombosis?
options: Initiation primarily by tissue factor exposure
Promotion primarily by platelet adhesion to injured vessel
Shared risk factors like age, obesity, and diabetes mellitus
items.explanation: Arterial and venous thrombosis share common risk factors such as age, obesity, cigarette smoking, diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.question: Which condition is associated with an increased risk of both DVT and cardiovascular disease due to elevated plasma levels?
items.explanation: Elevated plasma levels of Thrombin-activatable fibrinolysis inhibitor (TAFI) are associated with an increased risk of both DVT and cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.question: What is the primary mechanism for thrombosis in arteries?
options: Exposure of tissue factor leading to coagulation cascade activation
Adhesion of platelets to an injured vessel and exposure of extracellular matrix
items.explanation: In arteries, thrombosis is primarily promoted by the adhesion of platelets to an injured vessel and stimulated by exposed extracellular matrix.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW
items.statement: In venous thrombosis, primary hypercoagulable states are due to defects in blood flow or stasis.
items.explanation: Primary hypercoagulable states reflect defects in proteins governing coagulation and/or fibrinolysis.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW

items.subtopic: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW
items.statement: Approximately 805,000 Americans suffer from heart attacks annually.
items.explanation: The passage states that approximately 805,000 people suffer from heart attacks each year.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · Arterial and Venous Thrombosis | GENERAL OVERVIEW

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE
items.statement: Arterial thrombosis is a minor cause of morbidity and mortality in the United States.
items.explanation: The passage states that arterial thrombosis is a major cause of morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE
items.statement: Each year, about 795,000 people experience a new or recurrent ischemic stroke in the United States.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that each year approximately 795,000 people experience a new or recurrent ischemic stroke.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.explanation: Platelets are anucleate, meaning they lack a nucleus.

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.statement: The GPIb-IX-V complex is central to the initiation of platelet activation.
items.explanation: The GPIb-IX-V complex plays a crucial role in both platelet adhesion and the initiation of platelet activation.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.statement: Platelet activation leads to the formation of irreversible platelet aggregates.
items.explanation: Activated GPIIb/IIIa binding transforms aggregation from reversible to irreversible.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.statement: Hormonal replacement therapy decreases the risk of ischemic stroke and myocardial infarction.
items.explanation: Hormonal replacement therapy is associated with increased risks of these conditions.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Genetic variants in CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 are associated with increased platelet aggregability.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that specific alleles of these loci lead to increased platelet activity.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Genetic factors account for more than 50% of variability in clopidogrel's antiplatelet response.
items.explanation: The passage states that loss-of-function polymorphisms in CYP2C19 only account for 5–12% of the variability.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · GENETICS OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: The majority of venous thromboembolic events occur in locations other than the lower extremities or lungs.
items.explanation: The majority of venous thromboembolic events occur as pulmonary embolism or DVT of the lower extremities.

items.statement: The coagulation cascade involves a series of linked enzymatic reactions.
items.explanation: The coagulation cascade is defined as the formation of fibrin by a series of linked enzymatic reactions.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Homozygous protein C or protein S deficiencies are common and lead to mild thrombotic events.
items.explanation: Homozygous protein C or protein S deficiencies are rare and may lead to fatal purpura fulminans.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.statement: Nearly 20% of hospitalized COVID-19 patients were noted to have coagulation abnormalities.
items.explanation: Supportive of the increased risk, nearly 20% of hospitalized COVID-19 patients had coagulation abnormalities.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Factor V Leiden and prothrombin G20210A mutations are well-established risk factors for arterial thrombosis.
items.explanation: These thrombophilic conditions are known risk factors for venous thromboembolism but their contribution to arterial thrombosis is less well defined.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Inflammation plays a role in both coagulation-dependent thrombus formation and thrombus resolution.
items.explanation: Inflammation contributes to multiple overlapping thrombotic functions, including fibrinolysis, chemokine and cytokine production, and phagocytosis.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE
items.assertion: Platelets play a key role in arterial thrombosis.
items.reason: Arterial thrombus forms via platelet adhesion to the vessel wall and recruitment of additional platelets.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why platelets play a key role in arterial thrombosis.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS | ARTERIAL THROMBOSIS AND VASCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.assertion: Platelets can adapt to changing biological settings by altered gene transcription.
items.reason: Platelets lack a nucleus and cannot perform gene transcription.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because platelets, lacking a nucleus, cannot alter gene transcription.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 1)
items.assertion: Protease activation receptor 1 (PAR1) on platelets is activated by specific cleavage of the N-terminus by thrombin.
items.reason: Thrombin acts as a ligand for PAR receptors after cleaving their N-terminus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the mechanism of activation for PAR1.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.assertion: Platelets contribute to the pathophysiology of sepsis.
items.reason: Platelet activation and signaling pathways are involved in inflammatory responses during infections.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION
items.assertion: Diabetes is a classical cardiovascular-dependent risk factor for thrombosis.
items.reason: Classical factors include hypertension, high LDL cholesterol, and smoking.
items.explanation: The assertion is true but the reason does not correctly explain it as diabetes is not listed among classical factors.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · THE PLATELET (part 2) | THE ROLE OF PLATELETS AND THROMBOSIS IN INFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: Thrombin plays a central role in the coagulation cascade.
items.reason: Thrombin converts soluble plasma fibrinogen to insoluble fibrin and activates FXIII, which stabilizes the fibrin clot.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: Antithrombotic factors are irrelevant to the regulation of coagulation.
items.reason: Under normal conditions, antithrombotic factors limit thrombin production to prevent excessive clotting.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; antithrombotic factors are crucial in regulating coagulation.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · OVERVIEW OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS | DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | OVERVIEW OF THE COAGULATION CASCADE AND ITS ROLE IN VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: The risk of developing a VTE event doubles after air travel lasting 4 hours.
items.reason: The absolute risk remains low at 1 in 6000.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the doubling of relative risk.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · RISK FACTORS FOR VENOUS THROMBOSIS | GENETICS OF VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: Venous thrombosis is primarily initiated by the exposure of tissue factor.
items.reason: Exposure of tissue factor leads to activation of the coagulation cascade, resulting in thrombin formation and conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why venous thrombosis is primarily initiated by exposure of tissue factor.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: The metabolic syndrome is associated with a hypofibrinolytic state.
items.reason: This association is due to the increased levels of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) in individuals with metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; metabolic syndrome is associated with a hypofibrinolytic state primarily due to increased PAI-1 levels, not tPA.
items.reference: Arterial and Venous Thrombosis · FIBRINOLYSIS AND THROMBOSIS | THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN ARTERIAL AND VENOUS THROMBOSIS
